(vs St George Illawarra Dragons) | top_tries = | top_goals = | top_points = | prev_season = [[2010 Wigan Warriors season|2010]] | next_season = [[2012 Wigan Warriors season|2012]] | list = yes | list_link = List of Wigan Warriors seasons }}The '''2011 Wigan Warriors season''' is the [[Wigan Warriors|club]]'s 16th season in the [[Super League]], since its launch in 1996. [[Michael Maguire (rugby league)|Michael Maguire]] carried on as head coach in what would be his last season, and [[Ian Lenagan]] continued as the club's chairman and owner. Wigan reverted to using a solo captain in [[Sean O'Loughlin]], having used a "leadership team" during the [[2010 Wigan Warriors season|previous season]]. The club played at the [[Wigan|town]]'s [[DW Stadium]] for its 12th consecutive full season. Wigan entered the season as defending champions, having won the [[2010 Super League Grand Final]] against rivals [[St Helens R.F.C.]], and were pre-season favourites to do "[[Double (association football)|The Double]]" in defending their championship and winning the [[2011 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]]. They also had an opportunity as [[Super League XV]] winners to contest the [[2011 World Club Challenge]] at the DW Stadium on 27 February, but lost to [[St George Illawarra Dragons]]. Their regular season opened against St Helens RLFC on 12 February with a 16–16 draw. Their Challenge Cup campaign started in the fourth round on 8 May with a 52–0 [[Whitewash (sport)|whitewash]] over the [[Barrow Raiders]]. This was then followed up by a 28–22 win over fellow Super League side [[Bradford Bulls]], noted for the late challenge by Bradford's [[Gareth Raynor]], on [[Sam Tomkins]] that knocked the Wigan man out cold as he scored Wigan's second try and wins over [[Warrington Wolves]] and [[St Helens R.F.C.]] to set up a [[Wembley Stadium]] showdown with [[Leeds Rhinos]] on 27 August 2011.{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/tomkins-plays-down-vsign-row-after-wigan-roll-back-the-years-2345583.html|title=Tomkins plays down v-sign row after Wigan roll back the years|date=29 August 2011|work=Independent|accessdate=13 September 2011|archive-date=24 January 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120124192751/http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/tomkins-plays-down-vsign-row-after-wigan-roll-back-the-years-2345583.html|url-status=dead}} In the final itself, Wigan would run out 28–18 victors to record their 18th success in the competition and lift the trophy for the first time at the new Wembley Stadium.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/aug/27/leeds-rhinos-wigan-warriors-challenge-cup|title=Jeff Lima inspires Wigan Warriors to Challenge Cup win over Leeds|date=29 August 2011|work=Guardian|accessdate=13 September 2011}} ==Background== {{see also|History of Wigan Warriors}} [[File:DW Stadium.jpg|right|thumb|320px|The DW Stadium, Wigan Warriors home ground]] [[Ian Lenagan]] continued on as chairman of the club which achieved a 'B' license under the [[Rugby Football League]]'s [[2009–11 Super League licences|franchise system]] in 2008.{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/7519770.stm|title=Three clubs given A grade licence|date=23 July 2008|newspaper=BBC Sport|accessdate=13 November 2009}} Although Wigan's [[2010 Challenge Cup]] campaign ended in the quarter-finals following a loss to the [[Leeds Rhinos]],{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8708160.stm|title=Leeds beat Wigan to reach Challenge Cup semi-finals|newspaper=BBC|date=29 May 2010|accessdate=13 February 2011}} the Super League campaign ended with a 22–10 victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.]] in the [[2010 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] at [[Old Trafford]],{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/oct/02/wigan-st-helens-grand-final|title=Wigan turn St Helens into sinners with Super League Grand Final win|newspaper=BBC|first=Andy|last=Wilson|date=2 October 2010|accessdate=13 February 2011}} having already won the [[League Leader's Shield]] for finishing on top the Super League table at the close of the regular season.{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/7959030/Wigan-claim-first-League-Leaders-Shield-for-10-years-with-win-at-Hull-Kingston-Rovers.html|title=Wigan claim first League Leaders' Shield for 10 years with win at Hull Kingston Rovers|newspaper=The Daily Telegraph|date=22 August 2010|accessdate=13 February 2011}} This was the club's [[Wigan Warriors#Honours|second Super League title]], their first since [[Super League III]] in 1998. By virtue of becoming Super League premiers, the club was automatically entered into the [[2011 World Club Challenge]] to play the Australasian [[2010 NRL season|2010 National Rugby League]] premiers, the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]].{{citation needed|date=July 2020}} [[Michael Maguire (rugby league)|Michael Maguire]] entered his second year as head coach, despite speculation he would return to his native Australia,{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12215_6736950,00.html|title=Maguire – I'm happy at Wigan|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=7 February 2011|accessdate=13 February 2011}} following his debut year in which he brought the club's first major honour since [[Stuart Raper]] guided Wigan to success in the [[2002 Challenge Cup]] final.{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/1952991.stm|title=Wigan shock favourites Saints|newspaper=BBC|date=27 April 2002|access-date=13 February 2011| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20040729094712/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/1952991.stm| archive-date=29 July 2004| url-status= live}} Having used a "leadership team" consisting of [[Sean O'Loughlin]], [[George Carmont]], [[Thomas Leuluai]], [[Andy Coley]], [[Phil Bailey]] and [[Joel Tomkins]], the team opted to have O'Loughlin as their sole captain.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/jan/28/wigan-sean-oloughlin-single-captain|title=Wigan revert back to Sean O'Loughlin as single captain for 2011 season|newspaper=The Guardian|date=28 January 2011|accessdate=3 February 2011|first=Andy|last=Wilson}} The 2011 season was the club's sixteenth in the [[Super League]] as an ever-present side in the competition.{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/teams/Wigan_Warriors/seasons.html|title=Wigan Warriors – Seasons|work=Rugby League Project|accessdate=13 November 2009}} It was also the twelfth full season playing at the [[DW Stadium]], which the club had shared with [[Association football|football]] side [[Wigan Athletic F.C.]] since 1999.{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=37|title=DW Stadium – Facts & Figures|work=Wigan Warriors|access-date=16 February 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110314191638/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=37|archive-date=14 March 2011|url-status=dead|df=dmy-all}} The [[salary cap]] that all Super League XVI teams had to abide by remained unchanged from the previous year at [[Pound sterling|GBP£]]1.65 million, although a slight change was made for the 2011 season to provide more space for a club's own-trained players.{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8309498/St-Helens-captain-Paul-Wellens-issues-Super-League-salary-cap-plea-to-prevent-players-from-switching-codes.html|title=St Helens captain archive-date= 10 March 2011| url-status= live}} ==Pre-season== Following the completion of the domestic 2010 seasons in both the [[Super League XV|Super League]] and the [[2010 NRL season|National Rugby League]], the international season began with the [[2010 Rugby League Four Nations|2010 Four Nations]], this year held in Australia and New Zealand. [[Thomas Leuluai]] was the only British-based player to be named in the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand Kiwis]] squad for the tournament—[[Greg Eastwood]] played for [[Leeds Rhinos]] during 2010 and was also announced in the squad, but returned to [[Canterbury Bulldogs]] at the end of the season.{{cite news|last=Wilson|first=Andy|title=Wigan hero Thomas Leuluai only British-based player in Kiwis squad|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/oct/05/thomas-leuluai-new-zealand-wigan|accessdate=28 June 2011|newspaper=The Guardian|date=5 October 2010}}{{cite Goulding]], [[Sean O'Loughlin]] and [[Stuart Fielden]].{{cite news|title=Sam Tomkins named international rookie of the year|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/9149736.stm|accessdate=28 [[Test match (rugby league)|test match]] against [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoa]], while Tomkins was influential in England's 18–18 warm-up match against [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]], helping to set up a [[Ryan Atkins]] try and scoring one of his own.{{cite news|last=Brown|first=Michael|title=League: Kiwis seize control, thump Three pre-season friendlies were played as a warm-up to the 2011 campaign. Firstly Wigan faced [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] at [[The Willows, Salford|The Willows]], where a team largely consistent with the [[2010 Super League Grand Final|2010 Grand Final]] winning side, plus the additions of new arrivals Hoffman and Lima, was ultimately too much for Salford to hold back, with the Reds' head coach [[Shaun McRae]] describing Wigan as "the team to beat"{{cite news|last=Barker|first=Neil|title=Salford 24 Wigan 30|url=http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/1405474_salford_24_wigan_30|accessdate=29 June 2011|newspaper=Manchester Evening News|date=17 January 2011}} for the 2011 season. A young Wigan side then defeated the [[Barrow Raiders]] 24–32 at [[Craven Park (Barrow)|Craven Park]] in a [[testimonial match]].{{cite news|title=Raiders edged by Wigan in McDermott testimonial|url=http://www.nwemail.co.uk/raiders/raiders-edged-by-wigan-in-mcdermott-testimonial-1.801804?referrerPath=home|access-date=29 June 2011|newspaper=North West Evening Mail|date=23 January 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121008184311/http://www.nwemail.co.uk/raiders/raiders-edged-by-wigan-in-mcdermott-testimonial-1.801804?referrerPath=home|archive-date=8 October 2012|url-status=dead|df=dmy-all}} In a second testimonial, this time for [[Jon Clarke (rugby league)|Jon Clarke]], the Warriors won their final pre-season friendly against [[Warrington Wolves]].{{cite news|title=Warrington Wolves 18 Wigan Warriors 24|url=http://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/wolves/wolvesnews/8828981.FULL_TIME__PICTURES_ADDED__Warrington_Wolves_18_Wigan_Warriors_24/|accessdate=29 June 2011|newspaper=Warrington Guardian|date=2 February 2011}} {| class="wikitable" border="1" ! colspan="2" | LEGEND |- | style="background:#ddffdd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Win |- | style="background:#ffffdd" width=20 |   | width=100 | Draw |- | style="background:#ffdddd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Loss |- |} {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:95%;" width="50%" ! February 2011 |} ==Regular season== ===February=== [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]'s regular season started with a 16–all draw against derby rivals [[St Helens R.F.C.]] in front of a 30,891 crowd at the [[Millennium Stadium]]—a match in which three Wigan scores, including a debut try for [[Ryan Hoffman]], were cancelled out by three Saints tries late in the second half.{{Cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/9393298.stm|title=St Helens 16-16 Wigan|date=12 February 2011}} [[Jeff Lima (rugby league)|Jeff Lima]], who also made his debut during the first [[Round-robin tournament|round robin]] game of the season, received a two match ban for alleged [[chicken wing tackle]]s, meaning he missed the [[2011 World Club Challenge|World Club Challenge]] later in the month.{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12215_6754855,00.html|title=Lima banned for two games|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=15 February 2011|accessdate=16 February 2011}} However, a 0–26 half-time lead proved too much for [[Bradford Bulls]] as the Warriors won their next game comfortably, although [[Martin Gleeson (rugby league)|Martin Gleeson]] picked up a hamstring injury.{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8337082/Bradford-10-Wigan-44-match-report.html|title=Bradford 10 Wigan 44: match report|newspaper=The Daily Telegraph|date=20 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110310042007/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8337082/Bradford-10-Wigan-44-match-report.html| archive-date= 10 March 2011| url-status= live}} For the World Club Challenge, which was the first match of the 2011 season played at Wigan's [[DW Stadium]], [[Stuart Fielden]] had to be withdrawn from the squad on match day due to injury.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/feb/27/wigan-st-george-illawarra-world-club-challenge|title=Wigan Warriors make brave attempt but Dragons are still worlds apart|newspaper=The Guardian|date=27 February 2011|accessdate=27 March 2011|first=Andy|last=Wilson}} Gleeson was deemed fit enough to play and therefore started, but had to be withdrawn early against [[St George Illawarra Dragons]] because of his hamstring injury, a game which Wigan lost thanks to a [[Jamie Soward]] try despite the home side leading by one point at half-time. In the week preceding the World Club Challenge, the [[Manchester Evening News]] alleged that during the pre-season, Gleeson had been the target of [[blackmail]] which had included death threats, and that on 13 December 2010 he had contacted the police about the matter.{{cite news|url=http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/news/s/1409264_wigan_rugby_league_star_martin_gleeson_received_death_threats_in_blackmail_plot|title=Wigan rugby league star Martin Gleeson 'received death threats in blackmail plot'|newspaper=Manchester Evening News|date=25 February 2011|accessdate=27 March 2011|first=Mike|last=Keegan}} According to the newspaper, three men were arrested in relation to the claims, but Gleeson refused to assist the police further with their investigations and the case collapsed. ===March=== In their round four match, in which Lima made his return from suspension, a spirited [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] effort in the first half kept the visiting Wigan side to a six-point lead at half-time, but three-second half tries allowed the Warriors to pull away and put the World Club Challenge defeat behind them.{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_preview/0,19936,11070_3343362,00.html|title=City Reds v Warriors: Teams|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=4 March 2011|accessdate=27 March 2011}}{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3343362,00.html |title=Warriors battle past Reds 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=14 July 2011}} ===August=== August would bring 4 wins from 4 matches for the Warriors, extending their run to 12 consecutive wins. The month was bookended by triumphs in the Challenge Cup, with a hard-fought 18–12 win against St Helens in the semi-final and a 28–18 victory against Leeds Rhinos in the final at Wembley. In between, there were routine wins against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] (52–18) and [[Bradford Bulls]] (60–12). In the semi-final, Wigan survived an early onslaught by St Helens who couldn't convert early opportunities. Both sides had kicks at goal in the opening 20 minutes with Jamie Foster converting his to make the score 2–0 to Saints after 30 minutes. On 33 minutes a break from [[Josh Charnley]] down Wigan's right flank was stopped by Saints' [[Paul Wellens]] only for the Warriors to switch the ball to the other side on the next tackle for [[George Carmont]] to touch down. The game was still in the balance as both teams went in at 6–2 at the break. Wigan showed why they are the Champions after the break with two tries in the space of five minutes around the hour mark. The tie looked beyond doubt, however Saints fought back with two tries from Jamie Foster to make for a nervy finish. However, Wigan held out to book their place at Wembley at the end of the month. In Wigan's next game was at home to Salford City Reds. The shine was taken off the win as it was found that Ben Cross (who had only joined Wigan the previous month) had suffered a broken arm that would not only rule him out for the Challenge Cup final but also for the rest of the season, bringing his brief Wigan career to an end. Against Bradford, the Warriors were ruthless, running in 12 tries. Only the errant kicking boots of Pat Richards prevented the scoreline from being any higher.
